cards.title: How to Read Any Ortho X-Ray — ABCS Approach

alerts.label: Always request TWO views at 90° to each other (AP + lateral) — NEVER report on a single view
Additionally assess for fractures — describe using these 6 features
alerts.text: 1. Location (which bone, which third — proximal/mid/distal) · 2. Pattern (transverse/oblique/spiral/comminuted/avulsion/stress) · 3. Displacement (undisplaced/displaced — direction) · 4. Angulation (apex direction, degrees) · 5. Rotation · 6. Open vs closed (soft tissue integrity)

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Absence of fracture on X-ray does NOT exclude fracture. Scaphoid fractures are X-ray negative in 20% initially — if snuffbox tenderness present, treat as scaphoid fracture and repeat X-ray at 10 days (or MRI immediately). Stress fractures are also often X-ray negative for 2–3 weeks — request bone scan or MRI.

cards._legacyHtml:
Always request TWO views at 90° to each other (AP + lateral) — NEVER report on a single view
A
Alignment — Are the bones aligned correctly? Joint congruity maintained? Any dislocation or subluxation? Check articular surfaces are parallel and joint spaces symmetric.
B
Bone density & texture — Increased density (sclerosis = bone reaction, infection, avascular necrosis, metastasis). Decreased density (osteopenia, osteoporosis, lytic lesions from myeloma/metastasis). Trabecular pattern — interrupted = pathological fracture risk.
C
Cartilage / joint space — Measure joint space width. Narrowing = cartilage loss (osteoarthritis, inflammatory arthritis). Uniform narrowing = inflammatory. Focal/asymmetric = OA. Widening = effusion or ligament laxity.
S
Soft tissues — Swelling (joint effusion), calcification (CPPD, calcific tendinitis, myositis ossificans), gas (infection — surgical emergency), foreign bodies, fat pad signs.
Additionally assess for fractures — describe using these 6 features

Scaphoid fractures are X-ray negative in 20% initially — if snuffbox tenderness present, treat as scaphoid fracture and repeat X-ray at 10 days (or MRI immediately). Stress fractures are also often X-ray negative for 2–3 weeks — request bone scan or MRI.

cards.title: Fracture Classification & Patterns

rows: Horizontal fracture line perpendicular to bone axis
Fracture line at angle to bone shaft
Rotational/torsional force. Tibia in children = NAI (non-accidental injury)
Beware in children — report safeguarding concern
Vascular and nerve injury, complex fixation needed
Fracture ends driven into each other (increased density at fracture site)
Axial loading (fall on outstretched hand, heel)
May appear subtle — look for cortical buckling
Small bone fragment pulled away at ligament/tendon attachment
Sudden muscular contraction or ligament stress
Often invisible initially. Periosteal reaction ("grey cortex" sign) after 2–3 weeks
X-ray often negative — need MRI or bone scan
Fracture through abnormal bone — look for lytic/sclerotic lesion at fracture site
One cortex broken, other bent — like green stick

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Spiral fracture of femur in a child under 3 years = non-accidental injury until proven otherwise. Document carefully, involve paediatrics and safeguarding team. Do NOT send the child home without a full assessment.

cards.title: Common Orthopaedic X-Ray Findings — Upper Limb

rows: Distal radius fracture (within 2.5cm of wrist joint). Dorsal displacement + angulation = "dinner fork" deformity. Often with ulnar styloid avulsion.
Fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) in elderly osteoporotic women. Most common wrist fracture.
Distal radius fracture with VOLAR (forward) displacement — reverse Colles'
Fall on flexed wrist. "Garden spade" deformity.
Fracture line through scaphoid waist (most common). May be invisible acutely.
FOOSH in young adults. Snuffbox tenderness. Risk of avascular necrosis if missed (blood supply enters distally).
Ulna shaft fracture + radial head dislocation
"One bone fractures — the other dislocates." Always look at elbow when forearm fracture seen.
Radius shaft fracture + distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) dislocation
Converse of Monteggia. Check DRUJ when radial shaft fracture seen.
Fracture through distal humerus above condyles. Posterior fat pad sign ★ (sail sign) even without visible fracture line.
Most common elbow fracture in children. Risk: anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) and brachial artery injury → urgent reduction.
Anterior (95%): humeral head displaced forward and inferior — overlaps glenoid on AP. Loss of normal "light bulb" shape. Hill-Sachs deformity (compression fracture of posterior humeral head).
Always check axillary nerve function (sensation lateral arm) before and after reduction.
Widening of AC joint space (>5mm). Step deformity of clavicle. Grade I–VI (Rockwood).
Fall on tip of shoulder. Grade I–II conservative; Grade IV–VI surgical.
Amorphous calcium deposit in supraspinatus tendon (superior to greater tuberosity)
Severe acute shoulder pain. Calcium deposits may resorb spontaneously.

cards.title: Common Orthopaedic X-Ray Findings — Lower Limb

rows: Disruption of Shenton's line (smooth curve from femoral neck to obturator foramen). Valgus/varus angulation. Garden classification I–IV.
Elderly osteoporotic. Garden I–II = undisplaced (internal fixation). Garden III–IV = displaced (hemiarthroplasty/THR). AVN risk increases with displacement.
Fracture line between greater and lesser trochanters. Extracapsular. Comminuted in 4-part.
Extracapsular = better blood supply = lower AVN risk. Dynamic hip screw (DHS) fixation.
Fracture/depression of tibial plateau. Measure depression depth (>2mm = significant). Schatzker I–VI classification.
Axial loading + valgus force. Schatzker IV–VI = high energy = popliteal artery/peroneal nerve risk.
Weber A: below syndesmosis. Weber B: at level of syndesmosis. Weber C: above syndesmosis (syndesmosis likely disrupted).
Weber C = unstable = syndesmosis injury = surgery usually needed. Weber A usually stable.
Bohler's angle (<20° = flattened = fracture) and Gissane's angle (>130° = abnormal). Intra-articular subtalar involvement.
Axial loading (fall from height). Always check spine X-ray — 10% have associated spinal fracture.
Normally 2nd metatarsal base aligns with medial border of middle cuneiform. Any misalignment = Lisfranc. Fleck sign (small avulsion at base of 2nd MT) = pathognomonic.
Commonly missed! Weight-bearing X-rays show instability better. MRI for definitive assessment.
Klein's line (drawn along superior femoral neck) should intersect femoral epiphysis. In SUFE it misses the epiphysis (medial slip).
Obese adolescent boys. Urgent ortho referral — avoid weight-bearing. Bilateral in 25%.
Crescent sign (subchondral fracture), fragmentation, increased density of femoral head, widened joint space early
Boys 4–8 years. AVN of femoral head. Progresses over months to years.

cards.title: Arthritis on X-Ray — Quick Comparison (All Types)

alerts.label: Master rule
alerts.text: 5 features to check for every arthritis X-ray: Distribution · Joint space · Bone density · Erosions · Special features. The single fastest differentiator: DIPJs involved? → OA or Psoriatic. Spared? → RA.

rows: Knee, hip, DIPJs, 1st CMC, 1st MTPJ, spine
Symmetric MCPJs, PIPJs, wrists — SPARES DIPJs ★
Marginal "rat bite" erosions at bare area
Punched-out with overhanging sclerotic edge (Martel sign) ★
LOSS mnemonic: Loss of space, Osteophytes, Sclerosis, Subchondral cysts
Soft tissue swelling + ulnar drift + subluxation
Normal density + preserved space + overhanging erosion
Ivory phalanx, sausage digit, asymmetric SI
Bamboo spine, trolley track sign, fused SI joints

pearls.label: 🟡 One-line differentiators
pearls.text: OA = DIPJ + osteophytes + no erosions. RA = MCPJ/PIPJ symmetric + osteopenia + marginal erosions + spares DIPJ. Gout = 1st MTPJ + preserved space + overhanging erosion + tophi. PsA = DIPJ + pencil-in-cup + periosteal new bone. AS = bilateral SI joints + bamboo spine. CPPD = chondrocalcinosis in cartilage.

cards.title: Osteoarthritis (OA) — X-Ray in Detail

alerts.label: LOSS — the 4 hallmark X-ray signs of OA
alerts.text: Loss of joint space (asymmetric) · Osteophytes (bony lipping) · Subchondral sclerosis (↑ density) · Subchondral cysts (geodes)

rows: Medial compartment narrowing (most common), varus deformity, osteophytes at tibial spines and femoral condyles, subchondral sclerosis, patellofemoral OA (skyline view), loose bodies
Weight-bearing AP + lateral + skyline (patellofemoral). Standing films are MANDATORY.
Superior joint space narrowing (most common), femoral head and acetabular osteophytes, subchondral cysts, varus/valgus deformity, leg length discrepancy
Heberden's nodes (DIPJs — bony hard, non-tender), Bouchard's nodes (PIPJs), 1st CMC "squared thumb" appearance ★, asymmetric narrowing
PA and oblique views of hand
Disc space narrowing, anterior/posterior osteophytes, facet joint OA, vacuum phenomenon (gas in disc = degenerative), Schmorl's nodes (disc herniation into endplate)
AP + lateral. C5/6, L4/5 most common levels.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always take STANDING (weight-bearing) films for knee OA. A supine film underestimates narrowing by up to 2mm — a Grade II on supine may be Grade IV on standing. Orthopaedic surgeons require weight-bearing films before listing for knee replacement. Skyline (sunrise) view for patellofemoral OA — often missed on AP + lateral alone.

cards.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) — X-Ray in Detail

alerts.label: RA X-ray progression — early signs appear within 6 months

rows: Periarticular soft tissue swelling (fusiform), juxta-articular osteopenia ★ (FIRST sign), NO erosions yet, NO joint space loss
Marginal erosions at bare area ★, uniform joint space narrowing, wrist involvement, early subluxation
Large erosions, marked narrowing, joint subluxation (MCPJs — ulnar drift), swan neck, boutonnière deformity
Bony ankylosis (joint fusion), gross deformity, secondary OA superimposed
Symmetric MCP + PIP erosions, SPARES DIPJs ★, ulnar styloid erosion (earliest wrist sign), carpal destruction, ulnar deviation MCPJs
5th MTPJ erosion (earliest foot finding ★), hallux valgus, metatarsal subluxation
Atlantoaxial subluxation (C1-C2 gap >3mm = abnormal, >9mm = cord risk). ALWAYS X-ray cervical spine before GA in RA patients.
Uniform narrowing ALL 3 compartments (vs OA = medial only), valgus deformity (vs OA = varus), periarticular osteopenia

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Atlantoaxial subluxation in RA is a surgical emergency if symptomatic. C1-C2 distance on lateral flexion view >3mm = subluxation. RA patient with neck pain + hand tingling + leg weakness = urgent MRI cervical spine. NEVER manipulate the neck in RA without imaging. Inform anaesthetist before any GA.

cards.title: Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS) — X-Ray in Detail

alerts.label: AS progression: SI joints → lumbar → thoracic → cervical spine (bottom-up)

rows: Subchondral sclerosis (iliac side > sacral), pseudo-widening (erosions), blurred margins
Complete bony fusion — SI joints disappear
Shiny corners (Romanus lesion): erosion + sclerosis at anterior corners. Squaring of vertebral bodies (loss of normal anterior concavity).
Continuous calcification of annulus fibrosus + longitudinal ligaments. "Trolley track" sign on AP (3 vertical lines). Completely fused, brittle spine.
Bilateral symmetric narrowing (axial migration — different from OA which is superior)
Erosions + new bone at Achilles, plantar fascia, ischial tuberosities, iliac crests ("whiskering")

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Bamboo spine fractures are dangerous — the ankylosed spine behaves like a long bone. Even a minor fall can cause a complete unstable fracture through an ankylosed segment, frequently MISSED on plain X-ray. Always CT spine in AS patients after ANY trauma. The fracture pattern is typically through a disc level (3-column injury) — very unstable.

cards.title: Gout, Psoriatic & Other Arthropathies — X-Ray

rows: Punched-out erosion with overhanging sclerotic edge ★ (Martel sign / "rat bite with hood")
1st MTPJ ★, preserved joint space (even with large erosions), calcified tophi, normal bone density, asymmetric
Joint space PRESERVED despite large erosions — unique to gout
Chondrocalcinosis ★ — calcium lines in cartilage (knee menisci, wrist TFCC, symphysis pubis)
Acute: looks like septic arthritis or gout. Chronic: OA at unusual sites (wrist, MCPJs, shoulder, elbow)
Calcium in CARTILAGE (not soft tissue). Causes: ageing ★, hyperparathyroidism, haemochromatosis, hypomagnesaemia
Pencil-in-cup deformity ★ (whittling of one bone end fitting into cup-shaped erosion)
DIPJs ★, asymmetric, ivory phalanx (sclerotic distal phalanx), periosteal new bone, asymmetric sacroiliitis (vs AS = symmetric)
Both erosive AND productive changes on same finger. DIPJ + skin plaques = PsA.
Soft tissue swelling ★, joint space WIDENING (effusion)
Rapid progression to narrowing and destruction within days — faster than any other arthritis
Speed of destruction: septic = days. RA = months. OA = years.
5 Ds: Density ↑, Debris (loose bodies), Disorganisation, Distension, Dislocation ★
Grossly destructive X-ray with minimal clinical pain (disproportionate). DM (foot ★), tabes dorsalis (knee), syringomyelia (shoulder)
"Too much damage for too little pain" — gross X-ray destruction, unbothered patient
OA at unusual sites: 2nd and 3rd MCPJs ★ with hook-shaped osteophytes
Chondrocalcinosis of wrist TFCC and knee, osteoporosis
OA at MCPJs (unusual site) + chondrocalcinosis = haemochromatosis. Check ferritin + transferrin saturation.
Asymmetric oligoarthritis, calcaneal spurs ★, Achilles enthesopathy, periosteal reaction
Post-infectious (Chlamydia, Salmonella). Triad: urethritis + arthritis + conjunctivitis.

pearls.label: 🟡 Chondrocalcinosis causes mnemonic — ABCDEFGH
pearls.text: Ageing ★ · Biochemical (hypoMg, hypoP) · Calcium excess · Diabetes · Endocrine (hyperPTH, hypothyroid) · Familial (CCAL2 gene) · Gout (associated) · Haemochromatosis. In a young patient with chondrocalcinosis — always check calcium, PTH, magnesium, phosphate, ferritin, and transferrin saturation.

cards.title: Spine X-Ray Interpretation

rows: Bamboo spine (syndesmophytes bridging vertebrae), squaring of vertebral bodies, sacroiliac joint fusion (bilateral, symmetric), "trolley track" sign on AP
Forward slip of one vertebra on the other. L4/5 and L5/S1 most common. Scotty dog with collar = spondylolysis (pars defect).
Anterior wedge deformity, multiple levels, Schmorl's nodes (disc herniation into endplate)
Absent pedicle (winking owl), lytic or sclerotic lesion, vertebral collapse with posterior cortex involvement
Disc space narrowing (C5/6, C6/7 most common), osteophytes (foraminal narrowing), loss of cervical lordosis

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The "winking owl" sign: on AP lumbar X-ray, normal pedicles look like owl eyes. If one pedicle is absent or indistinct = metastatic destruction. This is often the earliest radiological sign of spinal metastasis. Always check pedicles systematically on every AP spine X-ray.

cards._legacyHtml:
1
Alignment — Check lateral view: normal cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis. Loss of lordosis = muscle spasm. Spondylolisthesis: one vertebra slips forward on the one below — grade by percentage displacement (Meyerding I–IV).
2
Vertebral body height — Compression fracture: anterior wedging of vertebral body. Height loss >50% = significant. Multilevel = osteoporosis. Single level with posterior involvement = metastasis/myeloma.
3
Disc spaces — Narrowed: degeneration, infection (discitis shows irregular endplates + narrowing + loss of definition). Widened: fracture-dislocation, infection early.
4
Posterior elements — Pedicles: absent pedicle on AP view = metastatic destruction ★ ("winking owl" sign). Facet joints: narrowing = degeneration. Spondylolysis: break in pars interarticularis (scotty dog sign on oblique view).
ConditionKey X-ray finding
Ankylosing spondylitisBamboo spine (syndesmophytes bridging vertebrae), squaring of vertebral bodies, sacroiliac joint fusion (bilateral, symmetric), "trolley track" sign on AP
SpondylolisthesisForward slip of one vertebra on the other. L4/5 and L5/S1 most common. Scotty dog with collar = spondylolysis (pars defect).
Osteoporotic compression fractureAnterior wedge deformity, multiple levels, Schmorl's nodes (disc herniation into endplate)
Metastatic diseaseAbsent pedicle (winking owl), lytic or sclerotic lesion, vertebral collapse with posterior cortex involvement
Cervical spondylosisDisc space narrowing (C5/6, C6/7 most common), osteophytes (foraminal narrowing), loss of cervical lordosis
🟡 Pearl
The "winking owl" sign: on AP lumbar X-ray, normal pedicles look like owl eyes. If one pedicle is absent or indistinct = metastatic destruction. This is often the earliest radiological sign of spinal metastasis. Always check pedicles systematically on every AP spine X-ray.

cards.title: Bone Density & Metabolic Bone Disease

rows: Cortical thinning, loss of trabecular pattern, vertebral "cod-fish" deformity
Radiolucent lines perpendicular to cortex, often bilateral and symmetric. Partial stress fractures.
Osteomalacia ★ (vitamin D deficiency, renal disease). Classic sites: femoral neck, pubic rami, ribs, scapulae.
Loss of cortical definition at radial aspect of middle phalanges, distal clavicle
Alternating dense and lucent bands in vertebral bodies — like a striped rugby jersey
Bone enlargement, cortical thickening, coarse trabecular pattern ("cotton wool" skull, "blade of grass" advancing front in long bones), bowing
Paget's disease of bone. Elevated ALP ★★★ (sometimes >1000 U/L)
Increased bone density in patchy distribution, may be multiple
Prostate cancer ★, breast cancer (osteoblastic), lymphoma. Contrast: lytic = kidney, thyroid, myeloma, lung
Punched-out, well-defined lytic lesions. "Soap bubble" = giant cell tumour. "Rain-drop" pattern skull = myeloma ★
Myeloma (skull), GCT (epiphysis of long bone), ABC, enchondroma

cards.title: Knee X-Ray — Detailed Assessment

rows: Doubtful narrowing + possible osteophytes. Mild OA.
Definite osteophytes + possible joint space narrowing. Mild OA.
Moderate joint space narrowing + multiple osteophytes + subchondral sclerosis. Moderate OA.
Severe narrowing (bone-on-bone) + large osteophytes + severe sclerosis + deformity. Severe OA = surgical candidate.
Skyline (sunrise) view: lateral patellofemoral joint space narrowing + osteophytes + patellar tilt
Dense fragment within joint space. OA, osteochondral injury, synovial osteochondromatosis.
Sesamoid bone in lateral gastrocnemius tendon. Seen in 10–30%. Normal variant — don't mistake for loose body or fracture.
Tibiofemoral angle on standing AP. Varus (bow-leg) = medial compartment OA. Valgus (knock-knee) = lateral compartment OA.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always take weight-bearing (standing) X-rays for knee OA assessment — non-weight-bearing films underestimate joint space narrowing by up to 2mm. A knee that looks grade II lying down may be grade IV on standing. Most orthopaedic surgeons require standing films before listing for knee replacement.

cards.title: Hip X-Ray — Assessment & Measurements

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The crescent sign in AVN: a thin radiolucent line just beneath the articular surface of the femoral head, representing a subchondral fracture. It is pathognomonic of AVN. MRI is far more sensitive for early AVN (before X-ray changes) — if clinical suspicion is high and X-ray is normal, MRI hip is the investigation of choice.

cards._legacyHtml:
Normal joint space
4–5 mm (superior)
Significant narrowing
<2 mm
CE angle (center-edge)
>25° (dysplasia if <20°)
Normal neck-shaft angle
125–135°
1
Shenton's line — Smooth arc from inferior femoral neck to inferior border of superior pubic ramus. Broken line = hip fracture or dislocation.
2
Acetabular coverage — Center-edge (CE) angle: angle between vertical line through femoral head centre and line to acetabular edge. <20° = dysplasia → risk of early OA.
3
Joint space narrowing pattern — Superior = classic OA. Medial = inflammatory (RA). Axial (all round) = avascular necrosis or inflammatory.
4
Avascular necrosis (AVN) signs — Crescent sign (subchondral fracture — pathognomonic), sclerosis, collapse and flattening of femoral head. Causes: steroids ★, alcohol, sickle cell, trauma, Perthes.
🟡 Pearl
The crescent sign in AVN: a thin radiolucent line just beneath the articular surface of the femoral head, representing a subchondral fracture. It is pathognomonic of AVN. MRI is far more sensitive for early AVN (before X-ray changes) — if clinical suspicion is high and X-ray is normal, MRI hip is the investigation of choice.

cards.title: Normal Echo Values — Must Know

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: EF is the most reported number but is the WORST measure of cardiac function in isolation. A patient with EF 60% can have severe diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF) and be very symptomatic. Always assess: EF + diastolic function (E/A ratio, E/e' ratio, LA volume) + wall motion + valve function together for a complete picture.

cards.title: LV Systolic Function Assessment

alerts.label: How EF is calculated
alerts.text: Simpson's biplane method (most accurate): traces LV at end-diastole and end-systole in A4C and A2C views. EF = (EDV - ESV) / EDV × 100

rows: Advanced DCM, end-stage IHD, decompensated heart failure. Consider ICD/CRT.

How EF is calculated

Normal≥55%Preserved systolic functionNormal or HFpEF (diastolic dysfunction)
Mildly reduced45–54%HFmrEFEarly cardiomyopathy, ischaemic heart disease

Regional wall motion abnormalities (RWMA) — Hypokinesia (reduced), akinesia (absent), dyskinesia (paradoxical outward motion in systole). RWMA in coronary artery territory = ischaemic heart disease ★.

cards.title: Diastolic Dysfunction — Grading

alerts.label: Why diastolic function matters
alerts.text: 50% of heart failure patients have preserved EF (HFpEF) — caused by diastolic dysfunction. Echo is the only non-invasive way to assess this. Always report diastolic parameters.

rows: Early — normal filling pressures. Often asymptomatic.
Elevated filling pressures. Symptomatic on exertion.
Severely elevated pressures. Severe HFpEF. Poor prognosis.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Pseudonormalisation (Grade II): E/A looks normal (0.8–2.0) but filling pressures are elevated. To unmask: Valsalva manoeuvre causes E/A <0.8 in Grade II (reveals impaired relaxation). Always check E/e' and LA volume — they don't pseudonormalise.

cards._legacyHtml:
Why diastolic function matters
50% of heart failure patients have preserved EF (HFpEF) — caused by diastolic dysfunction. Echo is the only non-invasive way to assess this. Always report diastolic parameters.
GradeE/A ratioE/e' ratioTR velocityLA volume indexInterpretation
Normal diastolic function0.8–2.0<14<2.8 m/s<34 mL/m²Normal filling pressures
Grade I — Impaired relaxation<0.8<14<2.8 m/s<34 mL/m²Early — normal filling pressures. Often asymptomatic.
Grade II — Pseudonormal0.8–2.0>14>2.8 m/s>34 mL/m²Elevated filling pressures. Symptomatic on exertion.
Grade III — Restrictive>2.0>14>2.8 m/s>34 mL/m²Severely elevated pressures. Severe HFpEF. Poor prognosis.
E wave = early passive filling (from LA to LV when MV opens). A wave = late active filling (atrial contraction). Measured by PW Doppler at mitral valve tips.
e' (tissue Doppler) = myocardial relaxation velocity at mitral annulus. Reduced e' (<7cm/s medial, <10 lateral) = impaired relaxation.
E/e' ratio = estimate of LV filling pressure. E/e' >14 = elevated LV filling pressure → likely HFpEF.
🟡 Pearl
Pseudonormalisation (Grade II): E/A looks normal (0.8–2.0) but filling pressures are elevated. To unmask: Valsalva manoeuvre causes E/A <0.8 in Grade II (reveals impaired relaxation). Always check E/e' and LA volume — they don't pseudonormalise.

Variable — may have stenosis or regurgitation

MR (mild to severe). Mid-systolic click on auscultation.
Billowing leaflet >2mm displacement. Barlow's disease if both leaflets thick and prolapsing.

cards.title: Pericardial Disease on Echo

rows: Anechoic (black) space around heart. Small: <10mm posterior. Moderate: 10–20mm. Large: >20mm. Circumferential vs loculated.
Causes: viral ★, malignancy, uraemia, hypothyroidism, TB, post-cardiac surgery, autoimmune

Large effusion + right atrial collapse (earliest, end-diastole) + RV diastolic collapse ★ + IVC plethora (>21mm, <50% collapse) + exaggerated respiratory variation in valve flows (>25% mitral, >40% tricuspid)
Clinical + echo diagnosis. Urgent pericardiocentesis needed. Beck's triad: hypotension + raised JVP + muffled heart sounds.
Thickened, bright pericardium (>4mm). Septal bounce (interventricular septal shift with respiration — "septal knock"). Dilated IVC. Normal or mildly reduced EF. Respiratory variation in E wave.

Post-viral, TB ★ (developing world), post-cardiac surgery. Treat surgically (pericardiectomy). Confused with restrictive cardiomyopathy — differentiate by hepatic vein flow reversal pattern and tissue Doppler e'.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Tamponade is a CLINICAL diagnosis — don't wait for echo confirmation if the patient is haemodynamically unstable (Beck's triad). The echo confirms and guides drainage. The earliest echo sign of tamponade is right atrial collapse (seen in systole), before RV collapse and before clinical deterioration. If you see it — the patient needs urgent intervention.

cards.title: Pulmonary Hypertension on Echo

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: D-shaped LV (interventricular septum flattens making LV look like a D instead of O on short axis) = RV pressure overload. Causes: pulmonary hypertension, massive PE, acute RV infarction. In PE: "60/60 sign" = RVSP 60mmHg + PA acceleration time <60ms = acute PH from PE (not chronic). Useful when CTPA not immediately available.

RV hypertrophy (RV free wall >5mm)

cards.title: USG Abdomen — Systematic Approach

alerts.label: Report structure — always describe all organs systematically

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always use BOTH longitudinal and transverse scanning planes for every organ. A lesion visible in only one plane may be artefact. Posterior acoustic shadowing (black shadow behind structure) = calcification/stone/gas. Posterior acoustic enhancement (bright area behind) = fluid-filled structure (cyst, gallbladder).

cards._legacyHtml:
Report structure — always describe all organs systematically
1
Preparation — Fasting 4–6 hours (reduces bowel gas). Full bladder for pelvic assessment. Patient supine, arms by sides. Use intercostal and subcostal windows.
2
Probe selection — Curvilinear (3.5–5 MHz): adults, abdominal organs. Linear (7–15 MHz): superficial structures, thyroid, testes. Phased array: cardiac, narrow windows.
3
Echogenicity terminology — Hyperechoic (brighter than reference — fat, calculi, gas). Hypoechoic (darker — fluid, most solid tumours). Anechoic (completely black — pure fluid, bile, urine). Isoechoic (same as reference — normal parenchyma).
4
Scan sequence — Liver → Gallbladder → Common bile duct → Pancreas → Spleen → Kidneys (both) → Aorta → IVC → Bladder → (Pelvis in women) → Free fluid (Morrison's pouch, splenorenal, pelvis)
🟡 Pearl
Always use BOTH longitudinal and transverse scanning planes for every organ. A lesion visible in only one plane may be artefact. Posterior acoustic shadowing (black shadow behind structure) = calcification/stone/gas. Posterior acoustic enhancement (bright area behind) = fluid-filled structure (cyst, gallbladder).

cards.title: Liver — Normal Values & Pathology

rows: Increased echogenicity (brighter than kidney and spleen), posterior attenuation (deep structures poorly seen), loss of vessel clarity. Graded I–III.
Grade I: mild. Grade II: moderate. Grade III: severe (posterior structures poorly seen, bright liver). Correlate with LFTs, metabolic syndrome.
Coarse, heterogeneous echotexture, nodular surface ★, shrunken right lobe + hypertrophied caudate lobe, splenomegaly, ascites, portal hypertension signs
Advanced chronic liver disease. Check for complications: portal HTN, ascites, HCC screening (6-monthly AFP + USS).
Anechoic, thin walls, posterior acoustic enhancement, no internal echoes. Round/oval.
Benign. No follow-up needed if <3cm with typical features.
Well-defined, hyperechoic (bright), homogeneous, usually <3cm. Posterior acoustic enhancement may be present.
Most common benign liver lesion. Confirm with MRI if >3cm or atypical features.
Variable echogenicity. Hypoechoic (common), heterogeneous in large tumours. Halo sign (hypoechoic rim). Portal vein invasion (tumour thrombus).
Elevated AFP. Confirm with multiphasic CT or MRI (arterial enhancement + washout = LI-RADS 5). Refer hepatology.
Multiple lesions, variable echogenicity. Bull's eye / target lesion (hyperechoic centre + hypoechoic halo) = classic. Often from GI, breast, lung primary.
Multiple hepatic lesions = metastases until proven otherwise. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging.
Focal hypoechoic/complex lesion with internal echoes and debris. Thick irregular walls. May have through-transmission.
Amoebic (single, right lobe, travel history) vs pyogenic (multiple). Fever, raised WBC, liver tenderness. Drainage ± antibiotics.
Portal vein >13mm, spleno-portal axis dilatation, splenomegaly, ascites, collateral vessels (para-umbilical, perisplenic, oesophageal), reversal of portal flow (hepatofugal)
Cirrhosis ★, portal vein thrombosis. Check liver echotexture.

cards.title: Gallbladder & Bile Ducts

rows: Hyperechoic foci within GB lumen + posterior acoustic shadowing ★ + mobile (move with position change)
Sensitivity of USS for gallstones: ~95%. Shadowing is the key — a stone without shadowing = artefact. WES triad: Wall-Echo-Shadow.
Thickened GB wall >3mm ★, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign (probe pressure over GB causes pain) ★, distended GB, gallstone often present
Sonographic Murphy's sign: 90% sensitive + specific for acute cholecystitis. Fever + RUQ pain + USS findings = urgent surgical referral.
Distended GB, internal echoes (pus), thickened wall, no acoustic shadowing within
Echogenic focus attached to GB wall, NON-mobile (fixed), NO shadowing
<1cm: benign polyp, reassure. >1cm or growing: refer for cholecystectomy (malignancy risk).
Irregular thickening of GB wall, soft tissue mass replacing GB, loss of plane between GB and liver, invasion of adjacent structures
Poor prognosis. Often found incidentally or late. Associated with gallstones, porcelain GB.
CBD >6mm (dilated). Intrahepatic biliary radicle (IHBR) dilatation (parallel channels — "double barrel shotgun" sign). May see cause: stone, mass at head of pancreas.
CBD dilation + jaundice = ERCP / MRCP urgently. Look for cause at head of pancreas (Ca head of pancreas) or CBD stone.
CBD >6mm + echogenic focus with shadowing in CBD. Often difficult to visualise (bowel gas).
MRCP is more sensitive (95%). ERCP for treatment (stone extraction).

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The WES triad for gallstones: Wall of GB → Echo (hyperechoic stone) → Shadow (acoustic shadow). All three together = 100% specific for gallstone. Acoustic shadowing is essential — a hyperechoic focus without shadowing = polyp, sludge, or artefact (not stone). Always confirm mobility by repositioning the patient.

cards.title: Kidneys — Normal Values & Pathology

rows: Bilateral small kidneys (<9cm), increased echogenicity (bright cortex = fibrosis), loss of corticomedullary differentiation, thinned cortex
Small echogenic kidneys = CKD until proven otherwise. Bilateral finding = systemic cause (diabetes, hypertension).
Normal or enlarged kidneys, normal or mildly increased echogenicity, preserved CMD. IVC may be flat (hypovolaemia) or distended (fluid overload).
Clinical correlation essential. USS helps exclude obstruction (post-renal AKI) — look for hydronephrosis.
Anechoic fluid in renal pelvis and calyces. Graded: mild (pelvis only), moderate (pelvis + major calyces), severe (cortical thinning).
Obstruction: stones ★ (look in ureter/VUJ/PUJ), BPH, malignancy, retroperitoneal fibrosis. Always look for cause.
Hyperechoic foci with posterior acoustic shadowing within renal parenchyma, pelvis, or collecting system. Twinkling artefact on colour Doppler.
Sensitivity 70% (lower than CT KUB). Small stones (<3mm) may be missed. Twinkling artefact on colour Doppler increases sensitivity.
Anechoic, smooth thin walls, posterior acoustic enhancement, no internal echoes.
Bosniak I–II: benign. Bosniak III–IV: complex, septations, calcification = refer urology (malignancy risk).
Heterogeneous solid mass, often isoechoic or slightly hyperechoic, distorts renal contour. May have calcification.
Haematuria + flank mass + flank pain = classic triad. Contrast CT for staging. IVC invasion possible.
Enlarged kidney, loss of CMD, focal hypoechoic areas (oedema/abscess). May appear normal in early disease.
Clinical diagnosis. USS useful to detect complications (abscess, obstruction) and exclude differential diagnoses.
Bilaterally enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts of varying sizes replacing normal parenchyma. Cysts also in liver.
Positive family history. Screen for berry aneurysm. Refer nephrology.

cards.title: Pancreas, Spleen & Aorta

rows: Enlarged oedematous pancreas (hypoechoic, poorly defined borders), peripancreatic fluid, loss of surrounding fat planes. Often poor visualisation due to bowel gas.
CT abdomen (contrast enhanced) is gold standard for assessment and Balthazar grading. USS: check gallstones as cause.
Atrophic pancreas, heterogeneous echotexture, calcifications ★ (bright foci with shadowing), dilated pancreatic duct (>3mm)
Alcohol ★ cause. Pancreatic duct dilation on USS = investigate with MRCP/ERCP. Risk of pancreatic cancer.
Hypoechoic mass in head of pancreas ★ (most common), dilated CBD + pancreatic duct (double duct sign), vascular invasion
Double duct sign = dilated CBD + dilated pancreatic duct = carcinoma until proven otherwise. Urgent CECT + CA19-9.
>13cm. Note: uniform (portal hypertension ★, haematological) vs heterogeneous (metastases, lymphoma, abscess, infarct)
Always report splenomegaly and suggest cause based on associated findings (portal hypertension signs, lymphadenopathy)
Infrarenal aorta >3cm (aneurysm), >5.5cm = surgical threshold. Intraluminal thrombus (echogenic crescentic material). Measure at widest point.

Pulsatile abdominal mass. Rupture risk increases sharply >5.5cm. USS screening for men >65 who have smoked. Surveillance intervals: 3.0–4.4cm = annual, 4.5–5.4cm = 3-monthly, ≥5.5cm = vascular surgical referral.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always scan the aorta in any patient with unexplained back/abdominal pain, hypotension, or pulsatile abdominal mass — ruptured AAA is rapidly fatal if missed. USS can confirm AAA in <2 minutes at the bedside. Measure the OUTER WALL to OUTER WALL diameter in transverse plane — do not include thrombus in the measurement of the true lumen only.

cards.title: Free Fluid & FAST Scan

alerts.label: FAST scan (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) — 4 windows in <2 minutes

rows: Transudate (ascites from portal hypertension, cardiac failure, nephrotic syndrome)
Acute haemoperitoneum (fresh blood is anechoic, clotted blood is echogenic)

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: FAST scan sensitivity for haemoperitoneum in trauma: ~80% (limited by operator skill, body habitus, bowel gas). A NEGATIVE FAST does NOT exclude intraabdominal injury — CT is required if clinical suspicion remains. In haemodynamically UNSTABLE patients with positive FAST → emergency surgery without CT.

cards._legacyHtml:
FAST scan (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) — 4 windows in <2 minutes
1
Morrison's pouch (RUQ) — Space between liver and right kidney. FIRST and most sensitive site for intraabdominal free fluid in trauma. Any anechoic fluid = positive.
2
Splenorenal recess (LUQ) — Space between spleen and left kidney. Also check splenodiaphragmatic space (fluid above spleen).
3
Pelvic (pouch of Douglas / rectovesical) — Probe suprapubic transverse and longitudinal. Most dependent area — small volumes collect here first in upright position.
4
Pericardial (subxiphoid) — Subcostal 4-chamber view. Anechoic fluid around heart = haemopericardium / tamponade. Extended FAST (eFAST) adds bilateral lung sliding assessment for pneumothorax.
Fluid appearanceLikely content
Anechoic (completely black)Transudate (ascites from portal hypertension, cardiac failure, nephrotic syndrome)
Low-level echoes (grey)Exudate, blood, pus, chyle
Complex (septations, debris)Abscess, haemoperitoneum (clotted blood), complex exudate
Echogenic clotAcute haemoperitoneum (fresh blood is anechoic, clotted blood is echogenic)
🟡 Pearl

A NEGATIVE FAST does NOT exclude intraabdominal injury — CT is required if clinical suspicion remains. In haemodynamically UNSTABLE patients with positive FAST → emergency surgery without CT.

cards.title: USG Pelvis — Uterus, Ovaries & Bladder

rows: Well-defined, hypoechoic, solid masses arising from myometrium. May be submucosal (distort endometrium), intramural, subserosal. Calcification in old fibroids.
Most common pelvic tumour in women. Submucous = heavy bleeding. Subserous = pressure symptoms. Refer if symptomatic.
Endometrial thickness >5mm in postmenopausal woman (especially with bleeding)
Endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. Hysteroscopy + biopsy. In premenopausal: thickness varies with cycle — correlate with LMP.
Anechoic, thin-walled, unilocular, posterior acoustic enhancement
<5cm in reproductive age = physiological / follicular cyst. >5cm or persisting: follow up. Post-menopausal: any cyst needs investigation.
Enlarged ovaries (>10mL volume), >12 follicles 2–9mm per ovary (necklace sign), increased stromal echogenicity
PCOS = PCO morphology + oligo/anovulation + hyperandrogenism (2 of 3 criteria — Rotterdam). USS alone insufficient for diagnosis.
Empty uterine cavity + adnexal mass (ring of fire on Doppler = trophoblastic flow) + free fluid in POD
Surgical emergency. Beta-hCG positive + empty uterus = ectopic until proven otherwise. Immediate gynaecology referral.
Hyperechoic fat component, calcification (tooth), "tip of iceberg" sign, Rokitansky nodule
Most common ovarian tumour in young women. Risk of torsion. Laparoscopic cystectomy.
Enlarged ovary (>5cm), peripheral cysts, absent or reduced Doppler flow, free fluid. Whirlpool sign (twisted pedicle) on Doppler.
Surgical emergency. Absent flow is late sign — torsion can occur with normal flow. Clinical diagnosis + urgent gynaecology referral.

cards.title: MRI Spine — Reading the Report

rows: Anatomy, bone marrow (fat = bright), haemorrhage (subacute), disc herniation
Spinal cord pathology ★, CSF (bright), disc (bright if normal hydration), nerve root compression, oedema
Bone marrow oedema ★ (stress fractures, metastases, discitis — bright signal in bone)
Tumours, infection (abscess ring enhances), post-op scarring vs recurrent disc (enhances = scar, doesn't = disc)
Posterior disc material beyond disc space margins. Graded: bulge → protrusion → extrusion → sequestration.
Correlate with clinical symptoms. Not all MRI disc herniations cause symptoms. Clinical + imaging together decide management.
Reduced AP diameter of spinal canal. <10mm (cervical) = severe stenosis. Trefoil configuration in lumbar stenosis.
Neurogenic claudication (lumbar): bilateral leg pain on walking, relieved by sitting/bending forward. Correlate with history.
Type I: T1 dark/T2 bright (bone marrow oedema — acute inflammation). Type II: T1 bright/T2 bright (fatty replacement — chronic). Type III: T1 dark/T2 dark (sclerosis)
Adjacent to degenerate disc. Modic I = active inflammation = may respond to treatment. Modic II = chronic/end-stage.
T2 signal change (bright) within spinal cord at level of compression = cord oedema/myelomalacia
Neurological emergency if acute. Surgical decompression needed. Do NOT manipulate spine if cord signal change present.
T2 bright + STIR bright at disc and adjacent endplates + loss of endplate definition + enhancing disc on T1+Gd
Spinal infection. Fever + back pain + elevated CRP/ESR. Blood cultures before antibiotics. Refer spinal team.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The most important MRI finding to never miss: T2 signal change WITHIN the spinal cord (not just compression — but cord signal change). This means myelopathy — the cord is being damaged. If this is present alongside clinical signs (upper motor neuron signs below lesion), urgent neurosurgical referral is required. Do NOT physiotherapy or manipulate until this is assessed.

cards.title: MRI Knee — Key Findings

rows: Homogeneously dark (black) on all sequences. Bow-tie appearance on sagittal view (peripheral meniscus), triangle on coronal.
Grade I: round T2 signal (doesn't reach surface — normal in young). Grade II: linear signal doesn't reach surface (degeneration). Grade III: signal reaches articular surface = TEAR ★
Dark, taut band coursing from posterior lateral femoral condyle to anterior tibial plateau. Steep Blumensaat's line angle.
Complete tear: discontinuity, T2 bright signal, lax/absent ligament. Partial: thinned, high signal. Secondary signs: lateral compartment bone bruise (pivot shift injury).
Dark, thick, arched band posterior to ACL. Normally has homogeneous low signal.
Tear: high signal, discontinuity. PCL injuries: dashboard injury (posterior knee impact).
T2 bright, smooth, uniform thickness over femoral condyles and tibial plateau.
Focal thinning, fissuring, subchondral bone exposed = OA. ICRS grading I–IV for cartilage defects.
Dark on T1, bright on STIR/T2 fat-sat
Bone bruise (trauma), AVN, stress fracture, subchondral insufficiency fracture (elderly women)
Fluid-filled cystic structure posterior to knee joint, in gastrocnemius-semimembranosus bursa
Secondary to intra-articular pathology (OA, meniscal tear). Treat the underlying cause.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: O'Donoghue's unhappy triad: ACL tear + MCL tear + medial meniscus tear from valgus + rotational force (tackled from outside in football). If you see a lateral femoral condyle bone bruise + ACL tear on MRI, always look carefully at the medial meniscus — the pivot shift mechanism commonly catches the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.

cards.title: DEXA Scan — Bone Mineral Density

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Artefacts that falsely ELEVATE DEXA T-score: aortic calcification (overlying spine), vertebral compression fractures (collapsed vertebra has higher density), osteophytes (spondylosis adds apparent density). A T-score of −1.5 in a 75-year-old with severe spondylosis may actually represent severe osteoporosis — lateral DEXA or forearm scan avoids this. Always read the DEXA report alongside the plain X-ray findings.

Severe osteoporosis
≤ −2.5 + fragility fracture
T-score = comparison to YOUNG HEALTHY ADULT peak bone mass. Used for post-menopausal women and men >50 years. One SD below peak = 10× increase in fracture risk.
Z-score = comparison to AGE-MATCHED peers. Use in premenopausal women, men <50, children. Z-score <−2.0 = "below expected for age" → look for secondary cause.
Sites measured — Lumbar spine (L1–L4) + proximal femur (femoral neck + total hip). Use the LOWEST T-score of any site for diagnosis. Forearm (radius) used if hip/spine not measurable.
FRAX tool — Integrates T-score + clinical risk factors (age, sex, BMI, previous fracture, steroid use, family history, smoking, alcohol, RA, secondary osteoporosis) to calculate 10-year fracture probability. Use FRAX to decide whether to treat.
Treatment threshold — FRAX 10-year major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20% OR hip fracture risk ≥3% = treat with bisphosphonate (alendronate first line). Always add calcium 1000mg/day + Vitamin D 800 IU/day.
🟡 Pearl
Artefacts that falsely ELEVATE DEXA T-score: aortic calcification (overlying spine), vertebral compression fractures (collapsed vertebra has higher density), osteophytes (spondylosis adds apparent density). A T-score of −1.5 in a 75-year-old with severe spondylosis may actually represent severe osteoporosis — lateral DEXA or forearm scan avoids this. Always read the DEXA report alongside the plain X-ray findings.

cards.title: ECG Interpretation — Systematic 8-Step Approach

alerts.label: Always use the same systematic approach — never skip steps
alerts.text: A systematic method prevents missing life-threatening findings hidden behind a seemingly obvious diagnosis.

pearls.label: 🟡 Complete all 8 steps every time
pearls.text: A patient with AF can also have ST elevation. A patient with LBBB may also have hyperkalaemia. "Spot diagnosis" ECG reading without a systematic approach misses 20–30% of significant findings. Complete all steps before concluding.

steps.title: Rate
steps.text: — Regular rhythm: 300 ÷ large squares between R-R. Quick method: 300→150→100→75→60→50. Normal = 60–100 bpm.

steps.title: Rhythm
steps.text: — Regular or irregular? Irregularly irregular = AF ★. Is every P followed by QRS?

steps.title: P wave
steps.text: — Present? Upright in I and II (sinus rhythm). Peaked = P pulmonale. Bifid = P mitrale (LAE). PR interval: 0.12–0.20 sec.

steps.title: PR interval
steps.text: — Normal 0.12–0.20 sec. Short (<0.12) = WPW/accessory pathway. Long (>0.20) = 1st degree HB. Progressive lengthening = Mobitz I (Wenckebach).

steps.title: QRS complex
steps.text: — Normal <0.12 sec. Wide QRS = BBB, VT, hyperkalaemia, paced rhythm. Axis: Normal −30° to +90°. LAD = −30° to −90°. RAD = +90° to +180°.

steps.title: ST segment ★
steps.text: — ST elevation ≥1mm limb leads / ≥2mm chest leads in ≥2 contiguous leads = STEMI until proven otherwise. ST depression = ischaemia, posterior MI, digoxin. Saddle-back vs coved = Brugada.

steps.title: T wave
steps.text: — Normally upright in I, II, V3–V6. Inverted = ischaemia, PE, RVH. Peaked = hyperkalaemia ★. Flat = hypokalaemia, hypothyroidism.

steps.title: QT interval
steps.text: — QTc = QT ÷ √(R-R in seconds). Normal QTc <440ms (men), <460ms (women). Prolonged QTc = Torsades risk.

cards.title: STEMI — Territory, Culprit Artery & Localisation

alerts.label: STEMI = ST elevation ≥1mm limb leads / ≥2mm chest leads in ≥2 contiguous leads. New LBBB with chest pain = treat as STEMI.
alerts.text: Door-to-balloon target ≤90 min. Every 30 min delay = 8% ↑ in 1-year mortality.

rows: LV failure, cardiogenic shock, LBBB, VT/VF
Hypotension with inferior MI — AVOID nitrates ★
ST depression + tall R/T in V1–V3 ★
Often missed — do posterior leads V7–V9

pearls.label: 🟡 Inferior MI + hypotension = RV infarction until proven otherwise
pearls.text: RV infarct occurs in 40% of inferior STEMIs. Triad: hypotension + clear lungs + raised JVP (Kussmaul's sign). ECG: ST elevation in V4R (right-sided lead). CRITICAL: NO nitrates — preload-dependent RV, nitrates cause catastrophic BP drop. Treatment: IV fluids 1L, primary PCI urgently.

cards.title: Arrhythmia Recognition — Quick Reference

rows: Normal P before every QRS — find the cause (pain, fever, PE, anaemia)
No P waves, chaotic baseline, variable R-R
Sawtooth flutter waves at 300/min. 2:1 block = ventricular 150.
Sudden onset/offset. P buried in QRS or pseudo-R in V1.
Wide complex tachycardia = VT until proven otherwise. Fusion/capture beats = VT.
No cardiac output → immediate defibrillation + CPR
Progressive PR prolongation → dropped beat. Benign, AV nodal level.
Sudden dropped QRS without PR change. Infranodal, high risk → pacemaker.
Present at own rate, no relation to QRS
AV dissociation — P and QRS march independently → pacemaker urgently

pearls.label: 🟡 Wide complex tachycardia — assume VT first
pearls.text: VT accounts for 80% of wide complex tachycardias. NEVER give verapamil to presumed SVT that is actually VT — can cause haemodynamic collapse and death. Haemodynamically unstable → synchronised DC cardioversion regardless of diagnosis. Brugada criteria help differentiate in stable patients.

cards.title: BBB (Bundle Branch Block) — LBBB vs RBBB

rows: Deep broad QS or rS (W pattern)
Broad tall R, no Q (M pattern)
Concordant ST ≥1mm · Concordant ST depression V1–V3 · Discordant ST ≥5mm → STEMI in LBBB

pearls.label: 🟡 WiLLiaM MaRRoW — fastest BBB mnemonic
pearls.text: WiLLiaM: W in V1, M in V6 = LBBB. MaRRoW: M in V1 (RSR'), W in V6 = RBBB. LBBB: look for M pattern in V5–V6 (tall broad R). RBBB: look for rabbit ears (RSR') in V1.

cards._legacyHtml:
RBBB criteria
QRS >0.12s · RSR' "rabbit ears" in V1 ★ · Wide S wave in I, V6
LBBB criteria
QRS >0.12s · Broad notched R in I, V5, V6 ★ · Absent septal Q in I, V5, V6
RBBB significance
May be normal variant. New RBBB + anterior chest pain = septal STEMI.
LBBB significance ★
Always pathological. New LBBB + chest pain = treat as STEMI. Masks ST changes.

cards.title: ECG in Electrolyte Abnormalities

rows: Peaked T waves → PR prolongation → P wave flattening → wide QRS → sine wave → VF. K⁺ >6.5 = cardiac emergency.
IV calcium gluconate FIRST (membrane stabilisation) → insulin+dextrose → salbutamol neb → dialysis
Flat/inverted T → U waves ★ (after T in V2–V3) → ST depression → prolonged QU → VT/TdP risk
IV/oral KCl. If prominent U waves → replenish Mg first (Mg required for K retention)
Prolonged QT (long ST segment) → TdP risk
IV calcium gluconate 10ml 10% slowly. Check Mg and K.
Short QT interval ★ · Short ST segment · Bradycardia · Osborn J waves (severe)
IV saline + furosemide. Bisphosphonates if malignancy-related.
Flat T, U waves, prolonged QT — causes refractory hypokalaemia
IV MgSO4 2g over 10 min. Must correct Mg before K replacement is effective.

pearls.label: 🟡 U wave = hypokalaemia hallmark
pearls.text: The U wave is a small deflection AFTER the T wave, best seen in V2–V3. Prominent U waves (taller than T wave) = significant hypokalaemia. Critical: the QU interval is prolonged in hypokalaemia — automated QTc calculations may incorrectly measure QU as QT. Always interpret QT manually in hypokalaemia.

rows: Sokolow-Lyon: SV1 + RV5 ≥35mm ★. ST depression + T inversion V5–V6 (strain pattern).
R > S in V1, RAD (>+90°), P pulmonale, ST depression V1–V3
PE, pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale, mitral stenosis

Viral, post-cardiac surgery. Treat: NSAIDs + colchicine.
S1Q3T3 ★ · Sinus tachycardia (most common) · New RBBB · T inversion V1–V4
S1Q3T3 present in only 20% — sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in PE
Low voltage ALL leads ★ · Electrical alternans (QRS axis alternates beat-to-beat) ★ · Sinus tachycardia
Beck's triad: ↓BP + ↑JVP + muffled heart sounds. Pulsus paradoxus >10mmHg.
Type 1 (diagnostic): Coved ST ≥2mm in V1–V2 + RBBB morphology ★. Type 2 (saddle-back) = not diagnostic alone.
Hereditary channelopathy. Risk of VF/SCD in young patients. ICD for symptomatic cases.
QTc >440ms men, >460ms women ★. QTc >500ms = high TdP risk.
Congenital or acquired (drugs: amiodarone, haloperidol, macrolides). TdP = polymorphic VT.
Short PR (<0.12s) ★ · Delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke) ★ · Wide QRS
NEVER give AV nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, digoxin) in pre-excited AF → VF risk
Osborn (J) waves ★ at QRS-ST junction in V4–V6 · Bradycardia · AF · Shivering artefact
J waves at <32°C. Rewarm slowly — risk of VF during rewarming.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pericarditis vs STEMI — the critical distinction
pearls.text: Both cause ST elevation. Key: (1) Pericarditis = global ST in ALL leads, no reciprocal changes + PR depression in II. (2) STEMI = regional ST with reciprocal depression elsewhere. (3) Spodick's ratio: ST:T amplitude in V6 >0.25 = pericarditis. (4) PR depression in lead II is pathognomonic of pericarditis. When in doubt → treat as STEMI until troponin and echo confirm.
